title: Method contracts and parameters

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Method contracts and parameters; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Classes and Objects, Arrays, and String processing learning paths; atomic topic: Method contracts and parameters
sources.claim: A Java method defines a typed call contract, receives copies of argument values, returns a declared value or completes void, and should make observable side effects explicit.

sources.claim: A Java method defines a typed call contract, receives copies of argument values, returns a declared value or completes void, and should make observable side effects explicit.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A Java method defines a typed call contract, receives copies of argument values, returns a declared value or completes void, and should make observable side effects explicit.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A static clamp method documents its inclusive bounds, returns a value for normal and boundary cases, and has no hidden mutation. Your goal is to implement and test methods while explaining Java's value-passing semantics. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Loops and branching; pf abstraction calls; Primitive types and variables; pf abstraction functions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A Java method defines a typed call contract, receives copies of argument values, returns a declared value or completes void, and should make observable side effects explicit. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: A copied reference still refers to the same object, reassignment of a parameter is local, mutable arguments can be changed through aliases, and exceptions are part of failure behavior. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Java passes objects by reference, so assigning a new object to a parameter also changes the caller's variable.

includes: Design static methods with cohesive purpose, typed parameters, return values and effects.
Implement and test methods while explaining Java's value-passing semantics.
excludes: A copied reference still refers to the same object, reassignment of a parameter is local, mutable arguments can be changed through aliases, and exceptions are part of failure behavior.

outcomes: Implement and test methods while explaining Java's value-passing semantics.

examples.title: Method contracts and parameters: direct language case
examples.situation: A static clamp method documents its inclusive bounds, returns a value for normal and boundary cases, and has no hidden mutation.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A static clamp method documents its inclusive bounds, returns a value for normal and boundary cases, and has no hidden mutation.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Java is pass-by-value: a reference value is copied for an object argument, so parameter reassignment stays local while mutation through the shared reference remains observable.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Java is pass-by-value: a reference value is copied for an object argument, so parameter reassignment stays local while mutation through the shared reference remains observable.

examples.title: Method contracts and parameters: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A method reassigns a List parameter without changing the caller's variable, but adding through that copied reference changes the shared list object.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A method reassigns a List parameter without changing the caller's variable, but adding through that copied reference changes the shared list object.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: A copied reference still refers to the same object, reassignment of a parameter is local, mutable arguments can be changed through aliases, and exceptions are part of failure behavior.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Java passes objects by reference, so assigning a new object to a parameter also changes the caller's variable.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and test methods while explaining Java's value-passing semantics.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Java passes objects by reference, so assigning a new object to a parameter also changes the caller's variable.
misconceptions.correction: Java is pass-by-value: a reference value is copied for an object argument, so parameter reassignment stays local while mutation through the shared reference remains observable.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Java passes objects by reference, so assigning a new object to a parameter also changes the caller's variable.

analogy.example: a set of labeled workstations that accept copies of request values and process fixed trays or immutable text tickets can introduce Method contracts and parameters, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: reasoning about method contracts, scope, arrays, and strings
limitations: reference values can still alias mutable objects and Unicode or overload rules have no exact workstation equivalent

recap.summary: You should now be able to implement and test methods while explaining Java's value-passing semantics. Keep this boundary in view: A copied reference still refers to the same object, reassignment of a parameter is local, mutable arguments can be changed through aliases, and exceptions are part of failure behavior.
